Section 19.14
MECHANISM OF ACID-CATALYZED ESTERIFICATION
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Esterification Revisited
We could imagine the carboxylic acid as nucleophile and the alcohol as electrophile or vice versa
These two mechanisms vary in the identity of the “non-carbonyl oxygen ”:
Experimentally, this oxygen comes from the alcohol
The alcohol acts as a nucleophile in this mechanism .

Mechanism of Esterification
Step 1: the carbonyl oxygen of RCO 2 H is protonated by acid
This step activates the carbonyl carbon as an electrophile !
A decent nucleophile, methanol, is a byproduct of this step

Mechanism of Esterification-1
Step 2: nucleophilic addition of the alcohol to the carbonyl carbon
The resulting tetrahedral intermediate contains the new C–O bond
Step 3: the intermediate is deprotonated, regenerating acid

Mechanism of Esterification-2
Step 4: one of the hydroxyl groups is protonated by acid
Water is now poised to act as a leaving group
Elimination of H + and H 2 O would give the ester product!

Mechanism of Esterification-3
Step 5: E1-like loss of water, giving a protonated ester intermediate
Step 6: proton transfer to ROH, regenerating acid

General Ideas of Esterification
Note that the carbonyl oxygen is protonated—not the hydroxyl oxygen!
We see three general stages in this mechanism:
Electrophilic activation of the carbonyl group
Nucleophilic addition
Elimination
These steps are common for carboxylic acid derivatives. We will see them again!

Lactones and Lactonization
Cyclic esters are called lactones
Lactone formation is catalyzed by acid and benefits from the large “effective concentration” of RCO 2 H and ROH

Naming Lactones
–oic acid in the parent carboxylic acid is replaced by –olide , and a locant identifies the oxygenated carbon
Macrolides are lactones with large ring sizes

Section 19.16
DECARBOXYLATION OF MALONIC ACID AND RELATED COMPOUNDS

Decarboxylation
Loss of CO 2 from a carboxylic acid is called decarboxylation
Decarboxylation of unactivated acids is very rarely encountered
Decarboxylation can be assisted by a nearby carbonyl group
Malonic acids undergo decarboxylation at high temperatures
β - ketoacids very easily surrender CO 2 to form ketones

Mechanisms of Decarboxylation
The nearby C=O group assists in deprotonation and loss of CO 2
Similar mechanism for R = alkyl (yields ketone, as above) and R = OH (yields carboxylic acid )

Section 19.17
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS

1 H NMR Spectroscopy
The carboxylic acid proton is strongly deshielded—the most deshielded of all commonly encountered protons
Adding D 2 O destroys this signal via H-D exchange

13 C NMR Spectroscopy
The carbonyl carbon of a carboxylic acid appears at δ 160 – 185, not quite as deshielded as ketone or aldehyde C=O

Mass Spectrometry
Molecular ion peaks are typically prominent
Minor fragment peak corresponding to loss of OH and formation of acylium ion
Minor fragment peak corresponding to loss of CO 2 H
